concept: Efficient / High Useful Output Relative to Selected Input
description: Of a process or system, producing a high proportion of a selected useful output relative to a selected input, resource, loss, or cost under stated conditions and within a stated boundary. By the quality-noun rule the word also names efficiency. Efficiency does not mean fast, productive, inexpensive, sustainable, fair, safe, sufficient, or good; changing what counts as useful or where the boundary is drawn may change the assessment.
sound_symbolism: The productive opening 'poru' moves into flowing 'seli', suggesting results carried from resources without the hard finality of perfection. Four syllables leave room for both sides of the comparison.
grammatical_notes: An adjective placed before a noun or used predicatively: 'poruseli terulora' (an efficient system); 'ha terulora poruseli nai.' (This system is efficient.) The quality-noun rule supplies efficiency. State the selected input, useful output, boundary, and conditions when the claim could mislead. Distinct from 'poru' (productive), 'thenoki' (sustain), and 'welao' (good).

pillars.solarpunk-values: Efficiency can reduce resource use, but the word explicitly keeps system boundaries, excluded harms, and chosen benefits available for ethical examination.
pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: The definition resists attachment to a favorable metric by asking what was counted, what was omitted, and under which conditions.

tags.activity: a relation between resources used and useful performance
tags.cognition: an assessment whose result depends on selected inputs, outputs, and boundaries
tags.creation: a comparative quality of designed processes and systems
